explicit_text: My mother, Anja Cain, was born in Berlin in 1966 and grew up in West Berlin during the Cold War. She experienced a sheltered and safe childhood despite the presence of American soldiers around her. Later in life, she moved to the United States with her family, adjusting to a new culture while raising her children. Although she loves her life here, she often feels like she doesn't quite fit in.

explicit_text: I grew up in West Berlin during the time when the Berlin Wall divided the city. Life there felt very safe and sheltered, and it was normal to see American soldiers around as part of the Cold War presence. Despite the tensions, I never felt uncomfortable walking around the city or going to school. West Berlin was my home during a very complex period in history.

explicit_text: The Berlin Wall was a constant presence during my mother's childhood in West Berlin. It physically and politically divided East and West Berlin, affecting her family deeply. Her parents were involved in resistance efforts against the Wall, which led to her mother's capture and imprisonment in East Germany. The Wall symbolized the division and danger that shaped their lives.

Uwe was involved in efforts to help people escape East Germany, which was dangerous and illegal at the time. He later married Regina and started a family in West Berlin.

explicit_text: My family moved to the United States in 2000, shortly after I was born. She appreciates the reverence for children in America and loves her life here, even though she sometimes feels like she doesn’t quite fit in.
